item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An out-of-context chunk of someone's life delivered way too straightforward. Where did this guy come from? What happened after? What is the Foundation going to do about it? Do the flowers keep yielding "fruit" every now and then, or are we left with a single instance of all those items? Do I even care?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel this is too on the nose. Aside from the use of the metaphor, there isn't really a connection between the plants and the events that took place. The story itself isn't particularly interesting, and the method of using snap-shot of items a la movie intro doesn't have anything going for it. In order for this to work as something more than a pun (or play of words, not sure what you call making a metaphor literal) the story should be subtle and have an intrinsic connection to gardening. SCP-2072 is a good example of this.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A short and interesting work, a very good way to tell a story.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely think that this one is a bit on the nose, but I like the exact story it tells. It's short, it's easy to understand, and it's sad. Excellent SCP here.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The objects telling a story is a bit on the nose. I mean do the flowers stop growing now that we've reached the end?

Also, giant plants don't really fit the tone. They should be normal sized plants with miniature items.

item.title: Re: JS Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
I generally hate tables in articles because it's just so, so difficult to actually make them further a story. You've done this beautifully here in creating a really sad implication with so few words and the faintest traces of prose.

This is extremely kind, I really appreciate this. I’ve want to do a table based scp with my normal heart-breaking tone laced throughout. Tbh I had a lot more in each but I was told that less is more.
I will be fixing the errors as soon as I reach my computer, unfortunately working on an SCP with tables on mobile isn’t a good idea (i messed up a whole draft while doing that)

item.title: JS Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
Hello author! I see you have a positively rated post but not much for feedback so I thought I'd drop in for some commentary.

This is a pretty short pieces and it works to your benefit because there isn't very much through either the Conprocs or the Description for anyone to latch onto. Both are very directly and simple, but they do a nice job of minimizing wasted attention. The story here is obviously in your table. Just wanted to highlight a couple of things.

have the ability to grow and blossom no matter the weather.

I supposed this phrasing is fine but the tone hits me a bit strange. Growing and blossoming is not really an "ability" in the context of the SCP, it's how plants function. The "ability" here is in doing in despite the weather so I guess I'd prefer this framed more around that. Something to the effect of…"is capable of blooming in spite of weather conditions or the local climate."

Your final paragraph in Descriptions feels like it's hanging on by its own a little bit. This doesn't detract from the piece very much, but this is just a very thin couple of lines that could have probably been baked into the primary paragraph or received a bit more support. I can't really fault how 'efficient' you are with your space and language though.

Which brings me to the crux of your piece. I generally hate tables in articles because it's just so, so difficult to actually make them further a story. You've done this beautifully here in creating a really sad implication with so few words and the faintest traces of prose. If there was a gripe to have it's that the table produces this information in the exact sequence necessary to maximize the impact of the story.

I don't know how else you are supposed to do it, and that's one of the drawbacks with this format, but in the same vein the weight of what you're saying is substantial enough that I really don't care.

This is a great, short piece with a lot of punch. Thanks for sharing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great way to tell a story in such a short and simple way. +1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Uncle Nicolini, geckoguy, OthellotheCat, and JackalRelated for crit. Anyways here's an edgy ass song to go with this: Good song
Image
